initial_diagnostic_question.question: Does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?

criterion_selection.question: To accurately diagnose complete left bundle branch block, which of the following diagnostic criteria should be evaluated?
options: Presence of an RSR' pattern in leads V1 and V2
Presence of a normal QRS duration

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, is the QRS duration prolonged on this ECG? Note that a QRS duration is considered to be prolonged if it is greater than 120 milliseconds.

grounding.question: In which of the following segments can you observe a QRS wave that demonstrates the prolonged QRS duration? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.
For the selected segment, which range does the measured QRS duration fall into?

decision.question: Based on the finding identified above, does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: In addition to the finding you just identified, which other diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose complete left bundle branch block?
options: Presence of a Delta wave (Slurred upstroke)
Presence of a qR pattern in leads I and aVL
Presence of a dominant R wave in leads V1 and V2
Presence of a dominant S wave in leads V1 and V2
Presence of ST depression in leads V1 and V2
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of a dominant S wave in leads V1 and V2

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show dominant S waves in leads V1 and V2, where the amplitude of the negative deflection in the QRS complex is greater than the amplitude of the positive deflection?

grounding.question: Within the leads V1 and V2, in which of the following segments can you observe the QRS complex with dominant S wave? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: In addition to the findings you have identified so far, which other diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose complete left bundle branch block?
options: Presence of a positive monophasic QRS complex without a Q wave in lateral leads
Presence of low voltage QRS complexes in lateral leads
Presence of pathological Q waves in lateral leads
Presence of negative QRS complexes in lateral leads
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of a positive monophasic QRS complex without a Q wave in lateral leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show positive monophasic QRS complexes without Q waves in at least two of leads I, aVL, and V6?

grounding.question: Which of the following leads show the positive monophasic QRS complexes without Q waves? Select all possible leads from the options below.

Within the selected leads, in which of the following segments can you observe the positive monophasic QRS complexes without Q waves? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: In addition to the findings you have identified so far, which other diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose complete left bundle branch block?
options: Presence of pathological Q waves in lateral leads
Presence of a notched R wave in lateral leads
Presence of a qR pattern in leads I and aVL
Presence of a slurred S wave in lateral leads
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of a notched R wave in lateral leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show notched QRS complexes in any of the lateral leads, including leads I, aVL, V5, and V6?

grounding.question: To verify that the lateral leads do not show a notched R wave, in which of the following segments can you locate the QRS complex in these leads? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: Since a notched R wave was not observed in any of the lateral leads, which alternative diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose complete left bundle branch block?
options: Presence of a deep S wave in leads V5 and V6
Presence of tall, peaked T waves in leads V5 and V6
Presence of pathological Q waves in leads V5 and V6
Presence of a broad monophasic R wave with a prolonged R wave peak time in leads V5 and V6
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of a broad monophasic R wave with a prolonged R wave peak time in leads V5 and V6

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show broad monophasic R waves, with an R wave peak time of at least 55 ms, in any of leads V5 and V6?

grounding.question: To verify that leads V5 and V6 do not show a broad monophasic R wave, in which of the following segments can you locate the QRS complex in these leads? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this ECG suggest the presence of complete left bundle branch block?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
